# Sources & references {.unnumbered}

All figures on this website come from public data. This appendix documents each
source, notes the main caveats, and lists full references (APA style) at the
end. Citation keys are given in brackets so they can be reused in the text.

## Data sources index

Every dataset the project uses (or is weighing). Raw files and a full provenance
record for each live under [`data/raw/<source>/`](https://github.com/donboyd5/ccs/tree/main/data)
in a tracked `SOURCE.md`; the scheme is described in
[`data/README.md`](https://github.com/donboyd5/ccs/blob/main/data/README.md).
Years are **school-year-ending** (2025 = 2024–25).

| Dataset | Publisher | What it provides | Years | Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Enrollment & staff | NYSED | K-12 enrollment; teacher/staff headcounts; average class size | 2016–2025 (staff 2018–2025; class size 2019–2025) | **In use** |
| Student test results (grades 3-8) | NYSED | grades 3-8 ELA & Math: % at/above proficiency, mean score, by district & subgroup | 2015–2025 (no 2020) | **Adding** |
| Pupil–teacher FTE (CCD) | NCES, via Urban Institute | FTE-based pupil-teacher ratio (cross-check) | 2016–2025 | **In use** |
| School district finance | NYS Comptroller (OSC) | revenue & expenditure by account code → per-pupil spending | 2013–2025 | **In use** |
| Audited financial reports | Federal Audit Clearinghouse | Cambridge CSD audited statements (PDF) | 2020–2025 | Reference |
| Property Tax Report Card | NYSED | proposed budget, tax levy & limit, projected enrollment | 2020–2027 | **Adding** |
| School district property taxes | NYS Comptroller (OSC) | tax levy, taxable full value & full-value tax rate, by district × town | 2015–2025 | **Adding** |
| Budget vote results | NYSED | budget vote & re-vote outcomes: defeats, contingency budgets, 60%-above-cap flag | 2012–2025 (vote yr) | **In use** |
| State school aid | NYS (data.ny.gov `9pb8-dg53`) | state aid to each district by year | 1996– | Candidate |
| Real property tax rates & levies | NYS (data.ny.gov `iq85-sdzs`) | tax levies and rates by municipality | varies | Candidate |

## NYSED data files

The enrollment and staffing numbers are built from two New York State Education
Department databases, both downloaded from
<https://data.nysed.gov/downloads.php> and distributed as annual Microsoft
Access files:

- **Enrollment database** [@nysed_enrollment] — the `BEDS Day Enrollment`
  table, which gives K-12 enrollment by district. Coverage on this website:
  school years **2015–16 through 2024–25**. Enrollment is the BEDS-day count
  (first Wednesday of October).
- **Student & Educator ("STUDED") database** [@nysed_studed] — the `Staff`
  table, which gives teacher, principal, counselor, and social-worker counts
  plus teacher attendance and turnover. Coverage: school years **2017–18
  through 2024–25**. Because teacher counts start in 2017–18, any measure that
  uses teachers (such as students per teacher) begins at school-year-ending
  2018. The same database's `Average Class Size` table — roster-based class
  sizes (students ÷ sections) for K-2 and the State-tested grade/subject and
  Regents courses, school years **2018–19 through 2024–25** — backs the
  class-size charts; it is measured from course rosters, not teacher counts, and
  NYSED's pre-2018-19 (teacher-form) class-size data uses a different method and
  is excluded.

These were stacked into a district-by-year panel by
[`src/build_enrollment_teachers.py`](https://github.com/donboyd5/ccs/blob/main/src/build_enrollment_teachers.py);
the dataset's own [SOURCE.md](https://github.com/donboyd5/ccs/blob/main/data/raw/nysed_enrollment_staff/SOURCE.md)
records the build in full.

**Caveats worth repeating.** `num_teachers` is a district-wide *headcount*
(all grades, not full-time-equivalents), while `k12_enrollment` is K-12 only.
The derived **students-per-teacher** ratio is therefore an approximate measure
for comparison across districts and over time — **not** NYSED's official
pupil-teacher ratio or average class size. Charter schools are excluded (they
have no district-total row), and a small number of districts reorganized within
the window (statewide, not in this comparison group).

## Student test results (NYSED grades 3-8 assessments)

To look at **student performance**, the project uses NYSED's annual **Report Card
Database** (also from <https://data.nysed.gov/downloads.php>, distributed as
Microsoft Access files). These databases hold the results of New York's **grades
3-8 English Language Arts (ELA) and Mathematics tests** — the statewide exams every
public-school student takes each spring — reported for each district (and for
statewide, county, and "similar-needs" peer groupings) and broken out by student
group. Coverage built so far: **school years 2015–16 through 2024–25** (ten years).
The same databases also contain Regents and other results, which may be added
later. The ten annual files were stitched into one district-by-year panel by
[`src/build_assessments.py`](https://github.com/donboyd5/ccs/blob/main/src/build_assessments.py);
full provenance is in
[`data/raw/nysed_report_card/SOURCE.md`](https://github.com/donboyd5/ccs/blob/main/data/raw/nysed_report_card/SOURCE.md).

The headline measure is the **percentage of students scoring at or above
proficiency** (performance Level 3 or higher); the data also include the number
tested and the average score. Two cautions matter a great deal for reading trends:

- **Spring 2020 is missing and 2021 is unusual.** The grades 3-8 tests were
  **cancelled in spring 2020** because of COVID-19, so that year has no data; in
  **spring 2021** only about half of students were tested, so 2021's numbers are not
  representative.
- **The tests were redesigned twice, so scores don't compare across the breaks.**
  New York set new tests and new passing standards in **2018** and again in **2023**
  (the Next Generation Learning Standards). Results are comparable *within* each of
  three periods — **2015–2017**, **2018–2022**, and **2023–2025** — but a change in
  the proficiency rate *across* one of those boundaries reflects the new standard as
  much as any change in student learning. Comparisons **between districts in the
  same year** are unaffected by this and are the most reliable use of the data.

## National pupil–teacher ratios (NCES Common Core of Data)

To cross-check the headcount-based ratio above against a full-time-equivalent
measure, the project pulls the federal **Common Core of Data** (CCD) school-
district directory — which reports teacher **FTE** — through the **Urban
Institute's Education Data Portal** (<https://educationdata.urban.org>), which
mirrors NCES. CCD labels a year by its fall term, so it is shifted to this
project's school-year-ending convention (CCD 2024 → 2024–25).

## School district finance (NYS Comptroller)

Per-pupil **spending** comes from the **New York State Comptroller's (OSC)**
local-government financial data — annual revenue and expenditure detail, by
account code, that every district reports in its Annual Update Document under the
state Uniform System of Accounts. Funds are identified by the leading letter of
the account code (General, Special Aid, School Lunch, Capital, Debt Service). The
**current operating spending per pupil** measure used in the spending chapter
keeps the General, Special Aid, and School Lunch funds and excludes capital
outlay, debt service, and transfers between funds, to approximate the U.S. Census
/ NCES basis for comparing districts. The fiscal year ends June 30 and matches the
school-year-ending label (no off-by-one). Dollars are **nominal** (not adjusted
for inflation). Full provenance:
[`data/raw/osc_school_finance/SOURCE.md`](https://github.com/donboyd5/ccs/blob/main/data/raw/osc_school_finance/SOURCE.md).

## Property Tax Report Card (NYSED)

NYSED posts a statewide **Property Tax Report Card** workbook each year
(<https://www.p12.nysed.gov/mgtserv/propertytax/>) recording, for every district,
the **proposed budget**, the **proposed and prior-year tax levy**, the district's
**tax levy limit**, **projected enrollment**, and reserve balances — the numbers
put before voters each May. These are *proposed/budgeted* figures (forward-
looking), in contrast to the Comptroller's *actual* spending. Provenance and the
year-by-year download list:
[`data/raw/nysed_property_tax_report_card/SOURCE.md`](https://github.com/donboyd5/ccs/blob/main/data/raw/nysed_property_tax_report_card/SOURCE.md).

## Budget vote results (NYSED)

NYSED posts statewide **School District Budget Voting Results** each year
(<https://www.p12.nysed.gov/mgtserv/votingresults/>): the May **vote** (every
district) and the June **re-vote** (districts that failed the first vote). From
these the project derives, per district and vote, the Yes/No counts and a
**60%-required flag** marking budgets above the tax cap (which must win a
supermajority). Together these identify budget **defeats**, **re-votes**, and
**contingency budgets** — the subject of the [Contingency budgets](../front/contingency-budgets.qmd)
chapter. These files carry district *names* (not codes) and **no levy figures**
(only the 60% flag), so they are not joinable to the district crosswalk; cap
status comes entirely from the flag. Provenance, the year-by-year URL list, and
the heuristic layout-detection parser:
[`data/raw/nysed_budget_votes/SOURCE.md`](https://github.com/donboyd5/ccs/blob/main/data/raw/nysed_budget_votes/SOURCE.md).

## School district property taxes (NYS Comptroller)

To show **how much property tax each school district raises and how heavily it
taxes property**, the project uses the Comptroller's **"Table 3" school-district
tax workbooks** (the same OSC local-government data program as the finance files,
at <https://www.osc.ny.gov/local-government/data/real-property-tax-levies-taxable-full-value-and-full-value-tax-rates>).
For every district and **each year 2015 through 2025**, they give the district's
**tax levy** (total property tax the district raises), its **taxable full value**
(the market-value tax base, after equalizing assessed values across towns), and
the **full-value tax rate** — the levy per $1,000 of full value, the standard way
to compare tax effort across places with different assessment practices.

A distinctive feature: each district's figures are **broken out by the city or
town the district covers** — many districts straddle several towns — so the data
has both a **detail row per district-and-town** and a **district total**. The
totals are summed from the parts (and match the Comptroller's own published
totals, where it publishes them, to the dollar). The fiscal year ends June 30 and
matches the school-year-ending label, the same as the finance data (verified
against the Property Tax Report Card). Districts are joined to the project's key
via a generated crosswalk; 678 of 680 link, the two exceptions being New York City
(which the state splits into many borough districts) and one non-operating
district. Full provenance, the format-change history, and caveats (including a
2019-file quirk) are in
[`data/raw/osc_school_district_tax_levies/SOURCE.md`](https://github.com/donboyd5/ccs/blob/main/data/raw/osc_school_district_tax_levies/SOURCE.md).

## Cambridge CSD annual financial reports

Cambridge's audited annual financial statements, for fiscal years ended
**June 30, 2020 through June 30, 2025**, were retrieved from the **Federal Audit
Clearinghouse** (<https://app.fac.gov/>). The clearinghouse was operated by the
U.S. Census Bureau through about 2022 and by the U.S. General Services
Administration from 2023 onward — which is why the earlier files (FY2020–FY2022)
carry a `CENSUS` tag and bear the title *"Financial statements and independent
auditor's report,"* while the later files (FY2023–FY2025) carry a `GSAFAC` tag
and the title *"Financial report."* They are the same series of district audits.

- FY2020 [@ccsd_fin_2020], FY2021 [@ccsd_fin_2021], FY2022 [@ccsd_fin_2022]
- FY2023 [@ccsd_fin_2023], FY2024 [@ccsd_fin_2024], FY2025 [@ccsd_fin_2025]

## Candidate sources under consideration

These are not yet used on the website but are likely additions, both from New
York's open-data portal (<https://data.ny.gov>):

- **State school aid** (dataset `9pb8-dg53`) — state aid to each school district
  by year, back to the mid-1990s. Would let the spending chapter separate
  state-funded from locally-funded dollars.
- **Real property tax rates & levy data by municipality** (dataset `iq85-sdzs`) —
  tax levies and rates by municipality, useful for putting each district's school
  tax levy in the context of total local property taxes.

## Reader comments

Each page of this website has a comment area at the bottom, powered by
[Giscus](https://giscus.app/). To leave a comment or a reaction, sign in with a
free [GitHub](https://github.com/signup) account; comments are stored as
discussions in this website's [GitHub repository](https://github.com/donboyd5/ccs).

## References

::: {#refs}
:::
